segments.text: Now the next Sutta is Angutra Nikaaya 10.11, the Buddha said,
Complete in five factors monks, a monk who follows after and resorts to the lodging place,
which is complete in five factors in no long time by destroying the asavas,
realizes in this same visible existence the release by mind, the release by wisdom,
thoroughly comprehending it of himself and having attained it abides therein.
I stopped here for a while.
So here the Buddha is saying that a monk should have five factors
and then he should stay in a lodging place, a dwelling, which also has another five factors.
Then he can attain the liberation by mind and liberation by wisdom.
So these are very important conditions if a monk wants to attain liberation
or in the realm of ebbs, these are the factors that he should know.
Just now I didn't mention by destroying the asavas. Asavas here they translate as kankas.
Asavas, I think a better translation is uncontrolled mental outflows, mind flows
because literally asavas means outflows and mental outflows means even the consciousness flowing.
It's because the consciousness keeps flowing and flowing.
That is why the whole world is created in our consciousness.
So when the asavas are destroyed, the mind doesn't flow anymore,
then the world doesn't arise in consciousness and that's the end of samsara around rebirths.
And then the Buddha continued, how is a monk complete in five factors?
Herein a monk is a believer or a monk has faith.
He believes he is the exalted one, arahan, samasambuddha, perfect in knowledge and practice,
a welfareer, a world-knower, trainer, unsurpassed, a man to be trained, teacher of deras and mankind,
awakened one, exalted one.
So this is the first factor the mum has to have faith that the Buddha is enlightened.
Then, moreover, he is little troubled by sickness and disease.
He is furnished with digestive power, not too hot or too cold, but even and suitable for striving.
Number three, he is honest, no deceiver.
He is one who shows himself as he really is to the teacher, to his discerning companions in the holy life.
Number four, he dwells resolute in energy, ever striving to abandon bad qualities,
stout and strong to acquire good qualities, not shirking the burden in good qualities.
Number five, he is a man of wisdom, possessed of wisdom, for seeing the rise and fall of things,
wisdom that is arahan, penetrating, going on to the utter destruction of ill.
In this way, a mum is complete in five factors.
And how is one's lodging place complete in five factors?
Here in mum's, a lodging place is not too far and not too near, but suitable for coming and going,
by day, not frequented, by night, quiet and undisturbed by noise,
not plagued with contact of flies, mosquitoes, wind, rain and creeping things.
Moreover, for one dwelling in such a lodging place, the supply of robes and arms,
food, or bent and lodging, comforts in sickness and medicaments, comes about with little trouble.
Further in that lodging place, dwell elder mums who have heard much, who are worst in the sayings,
who know the teachings thoroughly, who know the monastic discipline and summaries by heart,
consorting with them from time to time.
He inquires of them and questions them.
Thus, how is this your reverence? What is the meaning of this?
Those worthy then open up to him what was sealed,
make clear what was obscure, and on diverse doubtful points of doctrine, they resolve his doubts.
That mums is how one's lodging place is complete in five factors.
Indeed, mums complete in five factors, a monk who follows after and resorts to the lodging place,
complete also in five factors, in no long time, by destroying the asavas,
realizes in the same visible existence the release by mind, the release by wisdom,
thoroughly comprehending it of himself and having attained advice therein at the end of the sutta.
So, the five factors a mums should have is that first, he must have faith that the Buddha is enlightened.
Number two, he must be healthy, healthy and strong, not sickly, so that he can strive energetically.
Number three, he must be honest, honest to his teacher and to his companions.
He must not be the type of person who's always hiding his bad qualities.
He must show himself as he really is to his teacher, to his companions, so that they can help him by criticizing him.
We can only improve if we are willing to change ourselves.
If we are not willing to change ourselves, then we can never improve.
That is why this is important. We must hide our bad qualities.
Others know also that they can criticize us.
Number four, one must be energetic in striving to abandon his bad qualities and to acquire good qualities.
Number five, he must have wisdom to be able to see how things arise and pass away.
How things in this world all arise and pass away, that there is nothing stable in this world, a thing we can hold on to.
Everything has to arise and pass away.
So, these are the five qualities that mums must personally have.
Then he must stay in a place which has another five qualities.
Firstly, that lodging place must be a suitable place for a monk.
Like a forest monastery is very good.
If a monk has stayed in the forest by himself alone, he will find that if you stay in the forest by yourself alone, sometimes you are disturbed by insects.
Sometimes a place where you are sleeping, you might be sleeping on top of an ant nest.
In the middle of the night, you get bitten by ants all over and you can't even see properly.
So, sometimes you sleep on the ground, in the forest, a snake might come and crawl into where you are sleeping.
So, all these things could disturb you.
But if you sleep in a hut, in a forest hut, then you can practice much better.
That's why the forest monasteries are very suitable for striving because it has these qualities.
It's not too far, not too near and not too many people come during the daytime.
At night, it's quiet, etc.
And the second quality, the monk must be able to get the supply of requisites, food, robes, medicines and a proper dwelling place.
So, these must be suitable.
Number three, that place where he stays should have elder monks who have heard much.
Elder monks means Theras or Thero.
That refers to a monk who has taken the higher ordination at least 10 years or more.
And here he says that elder monks must be those who have heard much.
That means those who are very familiar with the suttas.
Why doesn't he mention those elder monks who are very good in meditation?
Here, this is more important.
If the monk is very familiar with the suttas, then he has the right view.
And if he has the right view, he can bring the other younger monks to have the right view.
And right view is the most important factor in the noble eightfold path.
And that's why it is put as the first factor of the noble eightfold path.
Only when you attain right view, have you entered the noble eightfold path?
And you have not attained right view, you have not entered the noble eightfold path.
Also, a person who has attained right view, he is already an Arya in the suttas.
A person who has attained right view is an Arya.
So once you are in an Arya, then you are already secure, you are safe,
you never fall into the woeful planes again.
It's only a matter of time before the Arya world becomes liberated.
That's why it's very important to be familiar with the suttas.
That's why if that monastery has senior monks who are much learned,
who know the suttas very well, and the discipline of Vinaya very well,
then they can teach the younger monks to have the right view.
So there is a third condition.
The fourth one, that monk must ask those senior monks what he doesn't understand.
He can inquire questions about doubtful points of the Dhamma and Vinaya.
And number five, those monks must be willing to resolve his doubts
by answering him, guiding him in the Dhamma and Vinaya.
So these are the five important conditions.
The first five important conditions that the monk must have.
Firstly, he must have faith in the enlightenment of the Buddha.
Number two, he must be strong and healthy so that he can strive.
For example, if you stay alone in the forest from monk to stay alone in the forest,
he has to stand cold. Sometimes he has to sleep on the bare floor.
The floor can be very cold. Sometimes he has to sleep in a cave.
He can have a wind blowing. If you're not strong enough, you cannot stand.
And sometimes you go and beg for the food.
Sometimes you might get food that does not suit you.
Sometimes you may not get enough food.
So if your body is not strong, you cannot stand all these things.
So that monk must be strong.
Then number three, he has to be honest, show himself as he really leads to his teacher
and to his companions so that he can get guidance.
Then number four, he must be willing to strive, energetic in striving.
And number five, he must have a certain amount of wisdom to attain insight.
So these are the five personal factors that he should have.
Then the other five factors should stay in a lodging place which is suitable,
which is quiet and undisturbed by not too many people come to disturb,
not too far, not too near, etc.
And number two, he requisites unnecessary food, ropes, etc.
He can get easily.
Number three, the dwelling place should have elder monks
who are much learned in the Dhamma and Vinaya
so can guide him in the Dhamma and Vinaya.
Number four, he should be able to ask them about Dhamma and Vinaya.
Number five, he must be willing to help him overcome all his doubts and all his questions.
So these are the five.
And the next sutra is 10.13.
The Buddha said, there are these ten fetters, monks.
Fetters is a translation for some eojana.
And these fetters are things that bind us to the world.
It's like in Chinese, I think the translation is so.
It's like a saw.
It's like something that is so low, so tall, so low.
So what are the ten fetters?
The five fetters leading to the lower world
and the five leading to the higher world.
And what monks are the five leading to the lower world?
The first one is Sakaya Diti.
Here they put the view of an individual group.
But nowadays some of the new translations,
they put the view of a personality view.
But I prefer to translate it as the view of a body being
or a being in the body.
I explain this afterwards.
The second one is Vici Kicha.
Skeptical doubt.
Number three is Silapata Parmasa.
Clinging to rules and rituals.
Number four is Kamaraga.
Sensual desire or sensual craving.
And number five is Vaya Pada or Vya Pada.
Ill will or malevolence.
These are the five leading to the lower world.
And what are the five leading to the higher world?
The first one is Rupa Raga.
Praying for fine material existence.
The second one is Harupa Raga.
Praying for immaterial existence.
Number three is Maana.
Conceit.
Number four is Udaksha.
Restlessness.
Number five is Avija.
Ignorance.
These are the five factors leading to the higher world.
What do you mean by five factors leading to the lower world?
That means they lead us to the sensual realm or desire realm.
The desire realm is the realm where sensual desire or lust
pervades all beings to have this sensual lust.
So the first factor is Sakaya Diti.
Sakaya Diti if you translate as personality view.
Personality view is like a view of a self, a view of a self.
But then according to the Suttas,
when a person attains Sota Panna, the first fruition, first fruit area,
he has already destroyed Sakaya Diti.
He has destroyed Sakaya Diti but he still has a self.
Only the Arahant does not have a self.
Even up to Anagami, they still have a self.
So this cannot be something like a view of a self.
To me, the view of a body being is probably more correct
because Sakaya could be made out of two words, Satta and Kaya.
Satta is being.
Kaya is body.
Why I say this?
Because in some other Sutta, the Buddha said,
it is easier for a person to see that this body is not the self.
It is very hard for us to see that there is no self even in the mind.
So to see that the body is not the self is easier.
Hence I think that a Sutta Panna could see that this body is not the self.
Although for most people, most ordinary people, Kutujanas,
they think that this body is the self.
So that is why I think that the Sakaya Diti means that
you can see that this body is not the self.
You still have a self even though you are in an area,
a Sutta Panna or a Sakadagami or anagamin.
So this Sakaya Diti is seeing that this body is associated with the self.
But when you break this, then you see that the body is not the self.
The second one is Vichikicha, skeptical doubt,
having doubt about the Buddha, Dharma and Sangha.
Doubting that the Buddha is enlightened.
Doubting that the Dharma is the true teachings and having doubt about the Sangha.
Having doubt about the training.
Having doubt whether this is wholesome or what is wholesome, what is unwholesome.
Having doubt whether this is the proper path to walk
or this is not the proper path to walk etc.
Only when a person has some understanding of the Dharma,
then he has no more doubt about the Dharma.
Then the third one is Silabatta Paramasa.
So you notice the first word is Sila, Silabatta Paramasa.
So this is clinging to rules and rituals.
So even rules, you could cling to it.
So that is why the Buddha said in some other sutra
that the Dharma Vinaya is for us to cross over on the other shore.
Suppose you needed to cross over to the other shore on the other bank.
So you make a boat or something.
And once you have crossed over, you don't still carry the boat on your head.
You put the boat down and you walk without the boat.
So what the Buddha is saying is that rules and rituals has a certain purpose.
So you must know what is the purpose of the rules and rituals and don't cling to it.
If you cling to it, then it becomes a show, making a show of it.
A state of understanding what it is for.
And then the fourth one is Kahmaraga.
Sensual craving or sensual lust.
The fifth one is Vyabhada, Ill-will or malevolence.
These are the five factors that bring us to the lower world,
the world of sensual desire and the desire realm.
And then the five higher factors.
The first one is craving for fine material existence, Rupa Raga.
Rupa refers to the form realm.
The realm where the rose who attained Jhana are reborn,
where the body is very fine, that's why it's called fine material existence.
So some people like to be reborn then.
Especially some people who don't understand the Dhamma, but they have attained Jhana.
That's why some of those early Indians, they want to be reborn as Mahabrahma.
This is craving for fine material existence.
The second one is craving for immaterial existence, Rupa Raga.
This craving to be reborn in the Rupa realm, the formless realm.
And then the third one is Conceit Mana.
Still having that conceit, conceit is like a self, still having that self.
And then the fourth one is Udaksha, restlessness.
And the fifth one is Avija, ignorance, ignorance about the Dhamma basically.
So all the distant factors, if a person has become, has attained the first path,
he has not destroyed any of the factors.
But in the suttas we find that normally a person attains the first path by listening to the Dhamma.
And after listening to the Dhamma and understanding the Four Noble Truths,
usually he has attained the first path.
Then the Buddha said, such a person is an Arya.
And before he dies, he will, that first path attained,
Arya will have attained the first fruition, suttapanna, before he dies.
Automatically, sometime or other before he dies, he must attain the first fruit.
So when the person becomes a suttapanna, he destroys the first three factors,
Sakaya Diti, the view of a body being.
Sivijikicha, sceptical doubt, and Silavata Paramasa,
clinging to rules and rituals, he will have destroyed.
That is suttapanna.
And then the Sakadagamin, the second fruit, Arya,
he would have destroyed this first tree as well as weakened sensual craving
and weakened ill-will, Vyapada.
So these two are weakened for Sakadagamin, the second fruit, Arya,
which means since this sensual craving and ill-will, Vyapada,
they are two of the hindrances.
If they are weakened, it probably means that the Sakadagamin
has a certain degree of concentration, not yet attained Jhana,
but he has a certain degree of concentration,
which enables him to weaken the sensual craving and ill-will.
He would have completely destroyed all these five lower factors.
Anagamin, the third fruit, Arya, he would have destroyed
Sakaya Diti, the view of a body being.
He would have destroyed sceptical doubt, destroyed clinging to rules and rituals,
destroyed sensual craving and destroyed ill-will.
That is anagamin, third fruit, Arya.
And then the arahan would have destroyed all the ten factors.
Even those leading to the higher world, the arahan has destroyed.
